comments.title: Spell for third eye
comments.content: Is it a good idea to get a spell cast to open your third eye?

replies.title: Re: Spell for third eye
replies.content:
No, not at all. If you just have someone cast a spell on you, without doing the necessary personal preparation and development, the best you can hope for is no effect at all, and the worst is a muddled hallucinatory state you can't control.

No, the way to do it is to tackle the necessary training yourself, so that you develop your clairvoyant powers in a balanced and deliberate way, that allows you to use them at will. There are quite a few good books on the subject; one I suggest as a starting point is W.E. Butler's How to Read the Aura, Practice Psychometry, Telepathy, and Clairvoyance, which has plenty of practical exercises suited to the beginning stages of the work.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG,

I consider myself Christian-leaning in religious orientation (more specifically, Orthodox) for a variety of reasons, but I disagree with certain of the dogmas and practices of the church. I can agree to them “provisionally”, but not without certain qualifications. For example, I have no problem admitting that God was made manifest in Christ, but not that this was a singular event, occurring once and only once in a particular corner of Palestine. I'm also inclined to seeing all religions as "vehicles" for reaching the same destination.

Can I still participate in a eucharistic ritual like the Divine Liturgy (equivalent to the Mass in Catholicism) and benefit from it, or would it be dishonest for me to participate while having reservations about core aspects of the teaching?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's something you have to settle with your own conscience. I don't recall Jesus himself saying anything to the effect of "Come all ye that are heavy laden, and I will give you peace, but only if you correctly answer a multiple choice test on theology" -- still, your mileage may vary.

One option you might consider is one of the alternative churches of the Independent Sacramental Movement, if there's one in your area. Many of them draw extensively on the Orthodox tradition but they don't fixate on theological correctness.

replies.title: Theological Correctness
replies.content:
Not OP.

This always seems to be a hard thing to deal with for those of us drawn to aspects of Christianity, but experience religious-cognitive dissonance when it comes to conforming to various (in)doctrine(ation).

How is it that Christianity became so focused on theological correctness as apart from spiritual experience? Some aspect of the Piscean age seems to be a part of it, as it can also be found in fundamentalist Buddhism and Islam, and many other places.

I realize people here don't have time to write a book on the subject, by the end of Magic Monday, which is what it might take.

The hard part for my conscience is not necessarily participating in liturgy (which I don't anyway, unless at a funeral or something) but dodging the daggers of those who like to engage in theological debate with the express purpose of forcing their particular theology.

To answer my own question perhaps joining Theo + Logia puts too much Logic into the mix. And while Logic may not have been at the root of the Greek word Logia, etymology wise, as the printing press and wider spread literacy availed more folks to read the Bible as the Age of Reason progressed, the Logical aspect of Theology may have prevailed to the point where chapter & verse references became more important than the reference of ones own lived experience.

Viridian Officious Lobster

replies.title: Re: Theological Correctness
replies.content: I'm not sure about the cause, but it's very much a habit of the prophetic religions of the Piscean era. I hope it goes away as the Aquarian age proceeds, because it's one of history's really classic bad ideas.

replies.title: Re: Theological Correctness
replies.content:
Synchronistically I just posted a related question on having a more complex relationship with the Christian god.

Your question reminds me of my own progress through The Doctrine and Ritual of High Magic. Here's Levi's thoughts on the cognitive dissonance you're facing:

"Do you wish to know which is the true religion? Look for the one which understands most about the divine order, the one which humanizes God and deifies man, the one which keeps the ternary doctrine intact, the one which incarnates the Verb by having the most ignorant among us see and touch God, and finally the one whose doctrine is suitable for all and can be adapted by everyone; the religion which is hierarchical and cyclical, which has for its children allegories and symbols, which contains for completed men a high philosophy, and has sublime hopes and sweet consolations for old men."

Focusing on the Piscean aspects of Christianity is like looking at a single frame of a movie. A religion is meant to be dynamic.

replies.content:
Not sure about the Orthodox, but the conditions for receiving the Host in Roman Catholicism are

1) to be a baptized Roman Catholic or Orthodox Christian. There’s a statement in the monthly missal that many parishes use, explaining that the reason members of other churches are not admitted to Communion is that doing so would imply a state of theological agreement and union that has not existed since King Henry VIII started his own religion, and that we should all pray for the situation to be set right. The separated brethren are welcome at Mass, though, and as a practical matter you won’t stick out if you don’t receive the Host, as a whole lot of Catholics who feel they’re not in a fit spiritual state to do so that week will be in the pews with you. No one will think anything of it. So join the worshippers at Our Lady of Perpetual Motion this weekend if you are so inclined, they’ll be happy to have you. Sit in your pew and enjoy the most spectacular magic most of us will ever encounter, stale wafers and indifferent wine becoming the body and blood of a god.

2) You should not be in a state of mortal sin, as receiving sacrilegious Communion will cause you no end of trouble in the afterlife if you don’t repent of same. A public figure who flagrantly flouts Church doctrine should be denied Communion (the rule Nancy Pelosi ran afoul of) until repenting, as that public figure is thus receiving sacrilegious Communions —not good for her—and possibly leading fellow Christians astray—not good for anyone. Despite his many charitable activities (seriously), Al Capone was denied Communion for most of his life because of this rule. Knowingly granting Communion to such a scandalous, as they’re called, public figure will leave the priest with some afterlife ‘splainin’ to do, as well.

Depending on how small-o orthodox your beliefs, this may or may not raise the awkward theological question of whether Pope Francis should be admitted to Communion. 😊. He seems like a nice old guy, but he does not seem to understand some basic Christian theology. ( I tend to view him as a liberal Protestant, myself.)

The religious education person at the nearest parish will be happy to answer questions on this or any other theological matter. Even better, religious education not being what it used to be, try to find a pre-Vatican II copy of the Baltimore Catechism.

—Princess Cutekitten

I'm not as familiar with Eastern Orthodoxy but most of my adult life, I've been a practicing Roman Catholic. I've been undergoing my own spiritual transformation as of late, and I no longer have any allegiance to the institutional leadership of Catholicism. But I've had to wrestle with what my baptismal vows, as well as my confirmation, mean for my relationship with the Christian god. After some discernment, I've been attending the Mass and still receive the Eucharist. I haven't felt any displeasure or anger from the Christian god, as though I shouldn't be doing this. That said, his presence has been minimal in my life, even before I became a polytheist. I might not be the best measure.

But in what experience I do have, the Christian god is far more loving and tolerant than the beliefs and practices of his churches would lead one to think. The discomfort that many feel at breaking with "orthodox" Christian beliefs and practices seems, in my own experience, to be a psychological issue that comes with the steady indoctrination that Christian leaders feed to their faithful. I've been "deprogramming" from that kind of thing. And the results have been spiritually beneficial. So, I don't think you need to have a guilty conscience for receiving the Eucharist as you are, and not as the Orthodox Church's leadership thinks you should be.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I gather from you're wording that you're not actually Orthodox (if you are, that seems like a question for your confessor!)?

From an Orthodox perspective, while you can't take communion, there's certainly a lot of benefit to be had from the liturgy and other services of the church, and it is not unusual to have people attend services for *years* while deciding (or not-deciding) whether they want to go all-in and join the church. One assumes all these folks have some non-trivial reservations, or they'd have joined up ages ago! As long as you're respectful, and don't participate in sacraments reserved to full members in good standing, it is not dishonest. It makes you an "inquirer".

In Orthodoxy particularly, we view salvation as a lifelong process of molding ourselves to God, not an "affirm these points of doctrine and sign here" sort of deal. It's not a set of intellectual checkboxes, but more a set of practices meant to shape us in a God-ward direction. We do not believe that God can be fully understood, or comprehended by the intellect, so... saying that you can't go to church because you can't intellectually affirm all its doctrines is kind of like, I dunno, saying you can't use a bicycle because you don't understand how gear ratios work.

At least from this layman's perspective, there's no problem with participating in services. We all start somewhere (when I started attending an Orthodox church, my residual Calvinist upbringing was in constant conflict with Orthodox practices. I went anyway). But you'd want to talk to a priest and resolve those questions and conflicts before becoming a member of the church. You do have to swear to uphold the doctrine of the church during chrismation-- so going through with that really *would* be dishonest and you should never swear to anything you don't actually mean.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
...and, as our Catholic commenter above notes, abstaining from communion won't make you stick out. Plenty of Orthodox do that, particularly if they haven't been to confession lately, or if they didn't fast before liturgy, or otherwise did not prepare themselves adequately for the eucharist. It's fine. If someone near you notices you didn't partake, they'll probably bring you a piece of the blessed bread. You can eat that, just don't throw it in the trash (rules for blessed objects apply).

It should be noted that we believe it is entirely possible to hold every correct belief and still not attain salvation. And that we have official venerated *saints* who've had portions of their writings rejected because they were kind of heretical. There are points of theology about which venerable church fathers disagree. Note also that the intellectually disabled, who could not dream of understanding, say, the debates that went on at the Council of Nicaea, are in no way barred from salvation. It's not about intellect. The ultimate question isn't whether you believe only correct things, it's whether you *know Christ*.

A whole lot of things were (by historical and cultural standards) suddenly implemented, including radically expanded vaccinations with poorly tested vaccines, which appear linked to health problems in the few real studies of them done; zero tolerance policies leading to things like kids being suspended for defending another child, or because someone else fought them; the transformation of schools into prisons over school shooting fears; the internet came to play a growing role in children's lives, with the host of negative side effects from that; the growing refusal on the part of parents, under the guidance of experts, to discipline their children; or the pressure which came from our parents looking to us to "save the world".

I was born in 1995, and have slowly come to realize that a lot of issues I have, and that my peers have, come back to the extremely toxic childhoods we had, many related to this insane system that was implemented. This raises a somewhat disturbing question though: what kinds of things should I look for in order to tell if there was some kind of involvement on the part of some kind of non-human intelligence in creating this system, whether that be a god, demon, or something else?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) No, you don't have to worry about it. It's heart center activation, and very often means you have a lot of unresolved emotional tension anchored in your chest muscles and that's beginning to relax.

2) Have you ever read about the experiments where scientists packed too many rats into too small a cage? The rats wigged out and started behaving in irrational ways, and one of the things they reliably did was harm their own young. That's what I think was going on here. When I was born, sixty years ago, the US had fewer than 200 million people living in it, and was already overcrowded by many measures; now it has 334 million -- it's the third most populous nation on the planet, right after China and India. Your generation had to bear the brunt of the resulting craziness, and I can well believe that it gave you a miserable and toxic childhood.

It's possible that nonhuman intelligences were also involved. Demons are recognizable by their weird illogic -- a sort of thinking I like to describe as "1 + 1 = petunia." When people behave in a way that literally makes no sense at all, even on its own terms, it's worth suspecting demonic involvement. Gods always make sense, by contrast, even if it's a terrible kind of sense: when they pass judgment, it's richly earned, and however baroque the penalty may be, it makes sense in terms of what's being punished. Those details may give you some clues. To know for certain, though, you'll need to develop your own spiritual senses, and that takes sustained practice of some system of personal spiritual training.

comments.title: Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose Qs
comments.content:
Hello!

I have a couple of clarifying questions about the daily work for the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose.

1. I know we are meant to be doing a daily divination. Is there any guidance for doing this, or - since I am still a Candidate preparing for the Seeker level - is that something I should engage more fully once I reach a more advanced grade? (At present I am pulling a daily single Tarot card from a deck that I particularly like, and making notes of its associations and interpretations. I'm not very skilled.)

2. For the discursive meditation, I feel as if I am having a lot of trouble with the posture. I tend to go deep into the thinking, and then find myself having trouble breathing - and it's because I've sort of slowly pitched forward and hunched over, so that my lungs are not open and I'm off balance. So I right myself - and then ever so slowly start to pitch forward again, without noticing, since I'm focusing on the theme. (I often had this problem in Zen meditation, too!). Do you happen to have any advice?

Thank you for any time and attention you give this!

replies.title: Re: Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose Qs
replies.content:
1) You can approach the daily divination in any way you like. As a Candidate, something very simple like drawing a single tarot card is a good practice; as you proceed, it would be wise to explore more complex readings, but one-card divinations are powerful -- John Gilbert used to insist that novice tarot readers should start with those, and go on to add other cards only when they'd learned to extract every drop of information from a single card.

2) One of the reliable things that happens to every novice meditator is that the unconscious mind rebels against the discipline of meditation. Some people have posture problems, some people have pains and cramps, some fall asleep, and the list goes on. The solution is to keep on practicing no matter what. You can learn the skill of keeping 5% or so of your attention on your posture while considering the theme -- it takes practice, but once you can do it, that background awareness can be useful for many other purposes. Keep at it!

Good evening, and I hope June is off to a good start for everyone here!

First, something to share:

a. I asked about works on aesthetics last week, and this week I'll try not to embarrass myself by asserting any opinions on poetry or "prose poetry" and share something that mostly drew recommendations for parodies of it (Kahlil Gibran's The Prophet for anyone following along at home).

At any rate, a couple of sources that I have found interesting are Wabi-Sabi and Wabi-Sabi: Further Thoughts by Leonard Koren. While "Wabi-Sabi" is not necessarily the aesthetic style I find most compelling, what I found most interesting about these works was the way that the author looked across many different fields to find the common aesthetic thread. If you're at all interested in understanding some of the less-obvious sides of traditional Japanese aesthetic sense, these books will be great.

For a question:

1. Somewhere, maybe in a past MM (that I can't seem to find), you and/or a commenter said something about blessings being the "common currency of the spiritual world" and talked about offering blessings to spiritual beings. When I first read this, I was a bit confused, which very likely comes from a misunderstanding or a more-or-less Christian upbringing, as I've always had the impression that one needs some kind of spiritual power or authority to offer a blessing, and that it might be presumptuous for normal folks to offer blessings willy-nilly.

So, I guess I'm a bit fuzzy on just what a "blessing" is and what qualifies someone to dispense one. Do you (or the commentariat) have any advice on how better to think about what blessings are, beyond well-wishes, and what role they have for normal folks?

comments.title: Categorical Confusion
comments.content:
Hi JMG. I've been listening to podcasts about neoplatonism while riding the bus to and from work. It seems every philosopher disagrees with every other philosopher about key details, though Iamblichus stands above all the rest.
A) What is the difference between magic ritual and religious ritual? Aren't both seeking to draw down the divine into our lives?

B) What is the difference between religion and magic?

C) What really is Theurgy? The original sources seem to define it as "The one and ONLY true path, which my philosophy school teaches, as opposed to those VERY BAD paths merely called "Theurgy" which are taught by competing philosophy schools!" Later sources seem to say that everything from the Catholic Mass to ordinary prayer is Theurgy, which seems watered down. Is "Theurgy" just an ancient buzzword?

replies.title: Re: Categorical Confusion
replies.content:
1 and 2) No two people seem to define these two words the same way. Not all magic ritual seeks to draw down divine energies, but some does, and there are religious rituals (such as prayers for rain) just as focused on practical goals as any form of magic could be. Treat them as vocal noises that different people use in different ways.

3) Here again, if you want to know what a given word "really is," what it really is is a glorified grunt -- a vocal noise humans use. In present-day magical practice, more often than not, theurgy refers to magical practices that seek to interact with divine forces for the purpose of individual spiritual development, while its opposite number thaumaturgy seeks to interact with a wide range of subtle forces (not all of them divine) for practical purposes.

comments.title: Grades Beyond Humans
comments.content: JMG - I think you have described humanity as a sort of grade that souls pass through.  Once souls pass this grade, what is next?  Is it always the same?  Or are there multiple possibilities?  And, might those souls return to humanity in a future incarnation?

replies.title: Re: Grades Beyond Humans
replies.content:
Human beings are fairly low on the scale of being. There are vast ranges of existence beyond us, with possibilities we literally can't imagine, and there's immense room for divergence. Think of it this way: if you're born as a skunk, your life is probably going to follow the usual skunk pattern, with only minor variations. If you're born as a human, you've got a lot more options in terms of what you do, where you go, and what experiences you have. When you proceed to the first level above the human stage, the range of possibilities open to you is as much greater than the human range as the human range was greater than the skunk's -- and when you rise beyond that level, the same rule applies again.

It very occasionally happens that a soul that's transcended the human level returns to human incarnation for some purpose. That's where messiahs and avatars come from.

replies.title: Re: Grades Beyond Humans
replies.content:
Skunks are apparently the theme of the day :)

I like to think of the first fish that flopped onto land. He marvels at the brand new world the intertidal zone represents. Yet he becomes aware of his shortcomings, and finds himself obsessing over things he never even considered before. "If only my fins were a bit stronger, I could make it further up the beach! If only I could cover my gills, I could be out there for longer!"

We're trained by progress to think of intelligence as going in one direction: up and to the right.

If only that little fish knew his descendants include reptiles, birds, mammals, and a weird group of primates flopping in their own intertidal zone...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
On 3)

You might find the Landmark Herodotus useful here - it's a very clear and scholarly translation of the main primary source on Thermopylae (and the rest of the Persian war) with extensive footnotes, introduction, timelines, maps, illustrations, and so forth.

Thermopylae is covered in Book 7, paragraphs 200-239. The account of their "gymnastics" and hair-combing is in 208, with Demaratos's (the exiled Spartan king) explanation to Xerxes of what they're doing in 209. Skimming it just now, there doesn't seem to be much on the spiritual significance right there, but I'm sure there's a lot more context in the rest of the work. There's also a Landmark edition of Thucydides, who necessarily has much to say on the Spartans, and it's likewise excellent.

On a more literary note, Gates of Fire by Steven Pressfield is a novelization of what happened at Thermopylae and contains a fictional, but compelling, depiction of the thoughts and beliefs of the Spartans at Thermopylae.

Also, I'll tag taylorrose, as he's shared some thoughts on physical culture and occultism in the past.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If I'm getting tagged, I guess I have to respond now :)

3. At the risk of being pedantic; the idea of calisthenics as worship isn't wrong, but it is highly misleading. I'm not familiar with any published English books that deal with this subject unfortunately. There are regular references to calisthenics and training throughout classical sources, and they collectively imply a great deal, but state very little directly. Probably the single most useful reference is in Plato's "The Republic" where he explains the value of physical exercise as part of a larger curriculum of mental and spiritual training.

As far as I can tell, the idea was that beauty is a basic principle underlying the world of appearances (cosmos). In calisthenics (beautiful strength) the body is brought to relate to itself and to a deeper underlying pattern in difficult but controlled circumstances; fortifying both the personal appearance (as in the word cosmetics) and the ability to interact with the larger world (the strength part). It is explicitly and repeatedly stated throughout classical literature that the physical training is simply the other half of mental training.

In my personal experience, treating exercise as a form of ritual and handling it with the same focus, conscientiousness, and intensity as a magical rite can have some really extraordinary effects.

replies.title: Re: WGS and DMH
replies.content:
1) They're pretty compatible, so it wouldn't be a matter of half speed, since the core practices (Sphere of Protection, discursive meditation, daily divination) are common to both.

replies.title: Herbs and plant ID
replies.content:
Depending on how into foraging you wanna get, I highly recommend getting both a good herb book, possibly targeted at your local area to start, and a good old field guide or two for plant ID. Weeds of the West is great in my area for well... Weeds! And you probably wanna get a local field guide targeted at plants that are not considered weedy.

If you wanna really get into botany and plant ID, I highly recommend Botany in a Day by Elpel. That will be a great textbook for learning how to key plants with your field guides, and recognize and identify plants by families. As well as teach you the plants you really need to avoid if you're gonna be going after wild plants. The only super lethal ones in my area are water hemlock, poison hemlock, and possibly monkshood. I would avoid foraging anything in the parsley family till you get good at plant ID, because of the hemlocks.

These are more food foraging books, but I also wanna plug The Foragers Harvest by Thayer, and Foraging the mountain west, co-authored by Elpel.

Best of luck to you!

-WindMan

comments.title: Empaths
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

Lately I've been meeting a lot of New Age people who describe themselves as empaths. Although ten years ago this label would have sounded intriguing and appealing to me, I've begun to feel decidedly skeptical about it.

More often than not, it seems, the self-identifier 'empath' is used to justify emotional dysregulation of various kinds and poor boundaries generally. Expensive energy healing sessions to restore balance are the default remedy. In short, I see the label 'empath' frequently functioning in much the same way that I've seen diagnoses of mental illness function among the non-spiritual: 'Oh, that's just the way I am, I just have to live with it, and it makes me special anyway, so why change it.'

I say this as someone who has overcome significant mental illness and emotional dysregulation in my own life. For me the key was to not get stuck self-identifying with my weaknesses, and to practice relentless self-reflection through journalling. My own sense of being special due to the intensity of my emotions was a hindrance rather than a help in healing. I had to constantly set it aside (not disproving or discounting it) and instead examine at every turn, 'What triggered me this time? How could this go differently next time? What can I do to gradually improve my overall resilience so this happens less often?' Important factors over the long term were diet, exercise, good habits and routines, positive social influences- all the boring and unglamorous stuff.

Although I have not undertaken the Octagon Society work, I find the compassionate yet saturnine approach to emotions taken by you and John Gilbert to be extraordinarily refreshing. Dion Fortune takes a similar tone in much of her writing. Based on these authors, it seems to me that 'feeling others people's feelings', the empath badge of honour, is not actually a special gift but the normal state of humans and animals existing amid the astral swamp. It's feeling one's OWN feelings and thinking one's own thoughts that is a sign of spiritual development.

Dion Fortune actually says the following in 'Psychic Self-Defense': "The psychic and the neurotic are closely akin in their reactions to life, but the neurotic differs from the psychic in that, instead of being over-engined for his hull, he is under-hulled for his engines. The result is the same, however, a discrepancy between the force and form with the consequent inability to maintain a central, reasoned, directing control."

How does this sound to you? Any qualifications or corrections you'd offer?

replies.title: Re: Empaths
replies.content:
I've been rolling my eyes at the marketing of the label "empath" as a self-identification for the emotionally self-indulgent. Now of course there are variations in how intensely people feel others' emotional states -- I know this from direct personal experience, because I'm at the opposite pole! That's a core element of Aspergers syndrome; I lack the mirror neurons that tell my brain what other people are feeling, so I really, truly don't feel your pain. ;-)

So I think there really are people who are intensely empathic -- who have mirror neurons that are more active than most -- but that doesn't change the fact that the concept's being misused as yet another way for people to excuse their own maltreatment of others. Of course even the most empathic people can learn to deal with their condition and behave in a polite and restrained way around others, just as people with Aspergers syndrome can learn to work around their deficits and function in society. (As indeed we can.)

Your comment that self-awareness and self-knowledge are essential to spiritual development is crucial, of course, and by and large, your comments seem very sensible to me.

replies.title: Re: Empaths
replies.content:
OP: Thanks. It's helpful to be reminded that there may indeed be people on the 'empath spectrum' just as there are on the 'autism spectrum'. One person I know comes to mind as an example, and I would not at all describe this person as irresponsible or dramatic.

The way I've seen the label abused is not so much for the purpose of maltreating others as for avoiding responsibility for one's own emotions. Self-indulgent, maybe, but I'd say more like self-avoiding.

I think the current and ongoing pandemic of fear we're witnessing in North America is one point of proof that feeling other people's emotions is not so rare as it's talked up to be. The average person can catch fearful emotions from the TV or the internet or just from being out in public, these days.

replies.title: Re: Empaths
replies.content:
There is a video, somewhere around, on a young female humourist on TikTok, speaking from the site of the concentration camp in Auschwitz, and saying: "as an empath, I feel that something bad happened here". It went around as a meme, and a lot of people ended up assuming it was sincere, as it is such an acute parody.

Empathy, though, is an interesting form of experience. One breakdown I have seen (via my friend Jasun Horsley, a writer who describes himself as on the autistic spectrum) distinguishes between cognitive empathy - the ability to rationally comprehend what other people are experiencing - and affective empathy - the ability to *actually* feel it.

On the one end, you have the psychopathic/sociopathic type, who can display high levels of cognitive empathy (think the cunning manipulator) but minimal to zero affective empathy. On the other end, according to this interpretation, you have the autistic type, who are able to feel deep levels of compassion, but may not find it so easy to rationally comprehend the emotional reactions of others.

The mainstream media often obfuscates this distinction, leading to a certain amount of defamation and hostility levelled at those on the autistic spectrum. One example would be the media surrounding the film We Need to Talk About Kevin, which featured a violent and sadistic adolescent, perfectly fitting the psychopath-type, who was described in interviews and reviews as "autistic".

Anyway, these are just some asides that might be of interest.

replies.title: Re: Empaths
replies.content: Horsley's distinction is quite sensible, but I think it works the other way as often as not. I had to learn to consciously read signals of other people's emotional states, because I don't feel those states.

replies.title: Re: Empaths
replies.content:
Thanks for that distinction, it clarifies something I was trying to grasp at when reading the initial comment and JMG's response. I can't say either that I'm clueless about what people around me are feeling, nor that I experience their feelings myself, I don't. It's an intellectual understanding, not an emotional response (and one that requires clear thinking on my part, so it works better when I'm at a distance from the person or after the fact when I've had a moment to turn it over in my head - in a heated exchange I might not have access to it and mistakes get made). I never went out of my way to learn this, but the upbringing I had was the sort that forces you to figure that out quickly.

That same upbringing makes me a pretty poor judge of what's normal for others. I know friends of mine that cry at movies would make jokes about me being soulless (all in good fun, lest anyone read this as a complaint, I was making jokes at them, too), I always just figured they were higher up the sensitivity scale and, while most women are much more emotionally sensitive than I am, there was nothing particularly unusual about my more intellectual understanding of these things. Was I wrong about that? Is it actually far more common for people to literally experience someone else's emotions, even in a small way?

(sorry if this is off topic for MM, but it came up so I thought I'd ask)

replies.title: Re: Empaths
replies.content:
OP again. I wonder if we've ended up identifying several separate phenomena here:

Cognitive empathy: the ability to read and consciously comprehend the emotional states of others

Affective empathy: the ability to reproduce the emotional states of others within one's own self through the activation of mirror neurons, which may occur consciously or unconsciously

Astral contagion: the tendency to 'catch' whatever mood is going around, usually unconsciously, unless one has developed the capacity to clearly and consciously perceive the astral plane

And each of these could be developed to a greater or lesser degree on the following spectrums:

Emotional Sensitivity: the depth to which one experiences emotions affectively

Emotional Balance: the degree to which emotions throw one off balance or cause one to 'lose it'.

comments.title: Loki query
comments.content:
Greetings, generous host and commentariat,

A group of young pagans of my acquaintance is planning on doing a blot to Loki for midsummer. They take him as a gender-bending god who fights oppression and speaks the truth. I'm not Heathen and haven't studied Norse mythology, so I wanted to ask those who are better versed in it whether this is a reasonable interpretation of Loki's behavior in the myths. I'm a Druid who's studied the Mabinogi, so I'm well aware that myths have meanings under the surface that aren't obvious at first.

replies.title: Re: Loki query

There is a lot of diversity of belief in the larger "Heathen" umbrella, so it's impossible to say what all Heathens believe about Loki, but in the myths he functions as a destructive, disruptive force. He often makes up for his tricks with something even better (Mjolnir, Gungnir, Gullinbursti, Asgard's walls, and much more all came from his tricks), but he also caused the death of Baldr and fights on the side of Surt to destroy the world at Ragnarok. If destruction, disruption, and disorder are what they want to invoke, that's their business I guess. As far as speaking the truth, I think Lokasenna(the episode where he roasts all the gods until Thor throws him out) is mostly full of invective and spin, and not really the "truth" - it seems to me more like an angry drunk venting than speaking truth to power. Btw, both Odin and Thor also cross dress or bend gender, so, there's that too.

That being said, he also is often the companion of Odin and/or Thor, and I don't think he's as terrible as some like to state.

comments.title: Feewill and artificial intelligence
comments.content:
Hello,

Can we have a brief overview of your current ideas about free-will and artificial intelligence?
Ok, I admit it's not directly related to the occult but close!

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Feewill and artificial intelligence
replies.content:
Free will has an enormous amount to do with magic, since magic is the art and science of causing change in consciousness in accordance with will. The crucial points to keep in mind about free will is that you don't get it for free -- you have to earn it -- and it's not absolute as long as you're incarnate. Most people have only a little bit of wiggle room when it comes to making their own choices -- they're pushed around pretty efficiently by biological and social pressures -- but you can expand your range of wiggle room through systematic exercise until you've got a fair amount of freedom.

As for artificial intelligence, cognitive scientists still haven't been able to agree on what intelligence is or how it functions, so I doubt they'll succeed in making it; most AI programs would better be described as artificial stupidity. I think it's quite possible that a computer system might be made complex enough, however, for a spirit to possess it; for complicated reasons, that's not something a benevolent spirit would be likely to do. Thus if they ever announce an artificial intelligence, it's a pretty good guess that what they'll actually have will be a computer possessed by a demon. No, that doesn't sound like a good idea to me, either.

replies.title: Re: Feewill and artificial intelligence
replies.content: Would you be willing to discuss why you doubt that a benevolent spirit would possess a computer, or point me in the direction of sources which would explain it?

replies.title: Re: Feewill and artificial intelligence
replies.content: It would require a couple of pages of explanation of occult philosophy. The very short form is that electronics works on what we can call the subnatural level -- the level of subatomic particles and quantum events -- and that level borders on the demonic.

replies.title: Re: Feewill and artificial intelligence
replies.content: By that definition then the Large Hadron Collider, the world's largest and highest-energy particle collider is demonic or could be a wonderful portal for demons.

replies.title: Re: Feewill and artificial intelligence

replies.content: I'll consider it.

replies.title: Re: Feewill and artificial intelligence
replies.content:
From a mythic/metaphoric perspective, it's the old Cain/Abel divide in the Old Testament. God likes shepherds more than farmers ;) but what happens with Cain? That's a really weird story. He kills his brother Abel, gets the boot from the original family, is given a mark by God so others won't kill him, and goes off and founds the first city. His descendants are "artificers"-- from thence comes technology and decorative art. Are those things bad? No, not in themselves. But... there's some ambivalence there, for sure! Those things are descended from Cain, who fell short in the eyes of God. Why? What, if anything, does that imply for progressively more complex technologies?

In addition, if you make a sort of mythic family tree there, you have Cain-->farmers-->cities-->technology, and Abel--->shepherds--->poetry, music, and divine inspiration(?)

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: Another perspective
replies.content:
jmg has already responded, and he's an initiated member of the order so, what he said is what it is.

That said, something that helped me figure out one or the other is the classification made by Giovanni Maciocia in his book The Practice of Chinese Medicine. In his view:

Guilt is an internal process. In its normative/physiologic form it what happens when you have broken a rule that your psique has successfully interiorized (you feel bad because, in your hearth of hearth, you know you have done badly). In its pathological form, which BTW is typical of overly strict Christian upbringing, the failure to measure up to an unreachable standard leaves you with the sense that you are bad. Period. You can feel guilty of situations or events that you had no control over.

Shame, on the other hand, is more related to our Social Self. Here we feel badly not necessarily because we believe we have acted badly, but because we are conscious that the action reflects poorly on how other people sees us. Just as guilt, shame can have both a normative (we follow not fully internalized rules because we care about our good standing in the community) and pathological forms (we are overly self-conscious and let other people have and undue say in our lives). According to Maciocia, all other things equal Guilt is more damaging than Shame.

comments.title: Novice Druid Questions
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I hope you’re having a lovely Monday so far. As a novice and student of Druidry, I figured there’s no one better out there to ask but the Archdruid Emeritus himself!

1. This coming Fall, I’ll be headed up to college for my freshman year. What advice do you have for a little sapling such as myself who still wishes to follow the Druid way, all while living in a crammed dorm room in college?

2. As we speak, I’m actually going on a little road trip with my dad; we’re following the historic Great River Road. We’re starting out in northern Minnesota at the headwaters of the Mississippi. Driving through this area, I notice a ton of Paul Bunyan stuff (on signs, roads, landmarks, locations, etc.), who I can’t help but associate with Hu the Mighty from our own Druid Revival traditions. The similarities are there: both are big, strong men, with interesting bovine companions, who like to wander around the countryside. Is there any chance that the American cult of Paul Bunyan could be a local manifestation of the Welsh Hu the Mighty?

3. I notice that several indigenous American cultures call upon God, the One, as the Great Spirit. I personally have taken a liking to this particular way of calling upon God. Is there any way that I could integrate this into my own Druid practice, and also (perhaps more importantly), do you think this would be seen as an act of cultural appropriation by indigenous peoples?

I graciously thank you for your time, Archdruid.

replies.title: Re: Novice Druid Questions
replies.content:
1) See if you can find a place outdoors where you can do your meditations and practices when the weather was right. When I was sharing a dorm room during my first year in college, I spent a lot of time up on the hill behind the college.

2) An interesting possibility! I don't know enough about the origins of the Paul Bunyan myth to be sure, but the archetype sounds similar.

3) The whole cultural-appropriation business has become a toxic mess of virtue signaling and one-upsmanship. My suggestion? Ignore it. If you like, you can translate the words "Great Spirit" into Welsh, Ysprydd Mawr (USS-bruth Mow-r, with the th pronounced as in "these clothes" rather than as in "thin myth," and the last word rhyming with "flower"), and nobody will know.

replies.title: Re: Novice Druid Questions
replies.content: 2. I think I read somewhere that Bunyan was the invention of an advertising company, way back in the day. But wouldn't that just be the most American thing ever, if a mythic being elbowed himself into public consciousness that way?

replies.title: Re: Novice Druid Questions
replies.content: That would be quite American! Just goes to show too that that there are far more places to find hints of the living divine other than in old folklore.

I am wondering about deities who fulfill some of the same roles as Mercury and Odin in the Druid tradition. I remember reading from (I think) Julius Caesar that Interpretatio Romana “Mercury” was one of the principle gods of the Gauls. I know you’re not a reconstructionist, but I was wondering if you might have insight on the ancient Celtic deities of this sort. But, I am especially interested how the modern tradition of Druidry views deities of this sort. I know that assigning gods to this role can be tricky and that “equivalents” may or may not be the same deity, even from gods with the same name from several thousand years ago, so I am more wondering about the roles filled rather than if it is the same entity.

A related question is how the ancient Roman gods relate to the planets. I think I remember you saying that you think it is possible that those gods were/are the consciousnesses inhabiting the planets. I think I remember reading somewhere that at least some of the ancients had this view in the Roman world. Do you know if other cultures in Europe and around the world held this view in ancient times? What did/do occultists think about that idea in the Renaissance and now?

Lastly, a bit of a related question: you have talked about the life cycles of the gods and how the ancient deities with particular names may well not be the same as those now. If the life span of a god is only a few thousand years, what about the entities inhabiting mountains, or even some of those ancient trees 5000-10000 years old? Do you think those are the same entities now as when they were young?

I know this is quite a lot to read through and I appreciate your time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) If I recall correctly, the theory is that the god the Romans had in mind when they talked about the Gaulish Mercury was Lugos, the same deity that comes down to us in Irish legend as Lugh, one of whose titles is Samildanach, "equally good at many arts." As for how modern Druidry sees this and other deities, why, it depends on the Druid; we don't do dogma, or for that matter consensus.

2) The worship of the planets as gods was a major theme in late antiquity, starting around the time of Plato and becoming pervasive as astrology spread out of Babylonia and became a common science all over the ancient world. A great many occultists from the Renaissance up to the present time have taken up the same concept. Of course others disagree!

3) Gods vary in their lifespans. Some last for just one precessional cycle, around 2160 years, but others last much longer. I don't happen to know about mountan and tree spirits.

comments.title: Aikido
comments.content: I have been thinking about taking up Aikido for years especially since I found out that it involves esoteric practices. With the whole MMA movement it seems like a lot of the traditional martial arts have been discredited as woo and I'd be better off joining an MMA gym from a practical standpoint, but then maybe the goal is not to go up against trained fighters in competition but to be able to get myself out of bad situations and learn some weapons handling. It seems like the binary here is between highly effective and spiritually empty vs spiritually fulfilling and questionable practical value. From what you have studied in martial arts does Aikido seem like a good compromise?

replies.title: Re: Aikido
replies.content: With aikido it depends very much on your teacher and your style. In Japan they teach aikido to police officers -- it's very practical -- but there are too many Western aikido teachers who have no practical skills worth noting and they've given the art a bad name. (Though they can still teach you the basics, and you can then go on to find someone to show you how to apply them.) If you feel an attraction to aikido, I'd say go for it, but be prepared for that to be the first step in a quest.

replies.title: Re: Aikido
replies.content:
I actually started martial arts with Aikido, though I will admit to having really mixed opinions about it. At a bare minimum, I doubt I would have stayed with it if I had started with something harder.

MMA vs. TMA is largely an exercise in stupidity and willful blindness. There is a huge amount of fraud and delusion in the TMA community that MMA has been helpful in cutting through. Unfortunately, MMA has also been aggressively insistent on removing huge amounts of useful material that may not be directly related to combat in a ring. The net result is that most martial artists end up having to choose between a truncated and incomplete martial art, or one that hasn't been tested under stressful conditions.

Regarding Aikido, it's philosophy when its understood and not subjected to babbling-hippy syndrome, is one of the more sophisticated options out there. With regards to the curriculum of movement it contains, it's a bit limited, but everything you need to be an effective combatant is there. Most impressive is the how many of the techniques pull double duty: most of the sword motions show up in joint locks, most of the footwork works as well for repositioning as dealing with a grappler, etc.

The downsides are pretty horrific too though. I honestly thought Aikido was a dead (and broken) art until I visited Japan. I trained in one of the better regarded Aikido schools in the US, and the quality of instruction was radically inferior to the Judo club closer to my house. A good Aikido instructor is actually hard to find in Japan too.

You should learn how to fall safely and develop some control over your body though, even in a substandard school.

Personally, I would recommend trying out different schools in your area. The school and the teacher are actually a bigger factor than the art. Mediocre classes you go to are better than excellent ones you don't.

comments.title: Railroad brotherhoods' occult connection?
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

Today was my first day of training for the railroad. There's obviously connections with fraternal societies within the different trades, but I'm curious what to look for to determine whether there may also be some occult connections? The language used to talk about the various labor jobs as "the Craft" struck me as a very likely connection, but could just as easily be attributed to the fraternal societies.

In any case, I'm thinking it may be worthwhile to go down some of the rabbit holes of the societies backgrounds and see where that leads me! Any tips, or connections you know of offhand, would be appreciated.

My heartfelt gratitude for all the work you do,
Prizm

replies.title: Re: Railroad brotherhoods' occult connection?
replies.content: I know that most of the old unions were founded by people with fraternal lodge backgrounds, and some of those have considerable esoteric background, but I don't know a thing about railroad brotherhoods as such. How fascinating!

replies.title: Re: Railroad brotherhoods' occult connection?
replies.content:
While the internet is still around and access to a lot of the various brotherhoods information available online, I sense I have an opportunity to start some research! Might make a book someone would find useful, who knows!

Prizm

comments.title: Difference between Tarot Decks
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Do you find that that the art/symbolism makes a significant difference when one is doing divination with a Tarot deck?

I have a RWS as well as a Thoth tarot, and I tend to interpret the same cards from each deck slightly differently when they turn up in a reading. While I appreciate both, The Thoth deck seems to know me better, and give less generic, more personalized answers to my questions.

What’s going on here? Is it something like my higher self, or whatever it is that is communicating with me via the cards finding more expressive through one versus another? (Like a multi-instrumentalist who can channel their energy better through a violin rather than a piano)?

Whenever I’ve been stuck with a reading, I found myself turning to the the Zalewskis’ “Magical tarot of the golden dawn” book as a resource. It’s proven very helpful, even though the symbolism it uses is slightly different from the RWS or Thoth decks I have. Is this a matter of the book implanting ideas in my subconscious, which whatever oracle is on the other side of the deck uses to communicate with me?

I look forward to my daily divination and find that the cards have helped me to be a better, more compassionate and thoughtful person. Seeing the energies of the cards arise in my life each day fills me with wonder. Thank you for helping me to develop this habit!

replies.title: Re: Difference between Tarot Decks
replies.content:
Different decks have different personalities. I've never been sure exactly how that works, but it unquestionably works. Back when I did a lot of tarot divination, I didn't use the RWS much, because it gave me generic readings; the Thoth was rude but relentlessly accurate -- I used it when I wanted the harshest possible portrayal of my actions or the nature of a situation. I mostly used David Palladini's Aquarian tarot, because it gave me good clear answers with a fair amount of subtlety.

Again, why it works is a really good question that I don't think anyone can answer for certain. The fact remains that it works.

comments.title: SOP
comments.content:
JMG,

After a laborious couple of months I am now doing the full SOP without consulting my notes. It definitely struck a cord the first time I did the full version, so thank you for that.

It's been a few weeks since then and I while I can feel a connection with air/fire/water/earth, I think my imagining for spirit below and above is lacking. For Spirit Below, I'm getting a "the floor is lava" + it's melt your face off hot. For Spirit Above, it's the coldest cold that ever colded + stainless steel & meat smell + "I'm floating in a tin can" playing in the background.

How can I learn what else is associated with Spirit Above & Below so that make connections between that and the imagery?
Or maybe you can offer more specific suggestions? Usually there's a bit of "exercise left to the reader" ethos, but if I don't know much about Spirit Above & Below, I'm not going anywhere in a hurry :)

I'm 1/3rd of the way through CosDoc. Would you associate Spirit Within w/ the seed atom or the Divine Spark?
Are there Spirit Above & Below connections?

BOb

replies.title: Re: SOP
replies.content: Spirit is always hard to grasp; our senses show us the elemental world, and so that's easy. The moral to this story is basically "relax." Open up a space for spirit above and spirit below to show themselves to you, keep doing the practice, and see what happens.
